Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - - VECODER VARIATIONAL EMBEDDINGS FOR COM MUNITY DETECTION AND NODE REPRESENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we study how to simultaneously learn two highly correlated tasks of graph analysis, i.e., community detection and node representation learning. We propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for jointly learning **V** ariational **E** mbeddings for **Co** mmunity **De** tection and node **R** epresentation. VECODER assumes that every node can be a member of one or more communities. The node embeddings are learned in such a way that connected nodes are not only “closer” to each other but also share similar community assignments. A joint learning framework leverages community-aware node embeddings for better community detection. We demonstrate on several graph datasets that VECODER effectively outperforms many competitive baselines on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection. We also show that VECODER is computationally efficient and has quite robust performance with varying hyperparameters.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graphs are flexible data structures that model complex relationships among entities, i.e. data points as nodes and the relations between nodes via edges. One important task in graph analysis is community detection, where the objective is to cluster nodes into multiple groups (communities). Each community is a set of densely connected nodes. The communities can be overlapping or non-overlapping, depending on whether they share some nodes or not. Several algorithmic (Ahn et al., 2010; Der´enyi et al., 2005) and probabilistic approaches (Gopalan & Blei, 2013; Leskovec & Mcauley, 2012; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2013) to community detection have been proposed. Another fundamental task in graph analysis is learning the node embeddings. These embeddings can then be used for downstream tasks like graph visualization (Tang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017) and classification (Cao et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2015).

In the literature, these tasks are usually treated separately. Although the standard graph embedding methods capture the basic connectivity, the learning of the node embeddings is independent of community detection. For instance, a simple approach can be to get the node embeddings via DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014) and get community assignments for each node by using k-means or Gaussian mixture model. Looking from the other perspective, methods like Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)), that focus on finding the community structure in the dataset, perform poorly for node-representation tasks e.g. node classification. This motivates us to study the approaches that jointly learn community-aware node embeddings.

Recently several approaches, like CNRL (Tu et al., 2018), ComE (Cavallari et al., 2017), vGraph (Sun et al. (2019)) etc, have been proposed to learn the node embeddings and detect communities simultaneously in a unified framework. Several studies have shown that community detection is improved by incorporating the node representation in the learning process (Cao et al., 2015; Kozdoba & Mannor, 2015). The intuition is that the global structure of graphs learned during community detection can provide useful context for node embeddings and vice versa.

The joint learning methods (CNRL, ComE and vGraph) learn two embeddings for each node. One node embedding is used for the node representation task. The second node embedding is the “context” embedding of the node which aids in community detection. As CNRL and ComE are based on Skip-Gram (Mikolov et al., 2013) and DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), they inherit “context” embedding from it for learning the neighbourhood information of the node. vGraph also requires two

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

node embeddings for parameterizing two different distributions. In contrast, we propose learning a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks. In this way, we not only get rid of an extraneous node embedding but also reduce the computational cost.

In this paper, we propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for jointly learning both community detection and node representation. The underlying intuition behind VECODER is that every node can be a member of one or more communities. However, the node embeddings should be learned in such a way that connected nodes are “closer” to each other than unconnected nodes. Moreover, connected nodes should have similar community assignments. Formally, we assume that for _i_ -th node, the node embeddings _**z** i_ are generated from a prior distribution _p_ ( _**z**_ ). Given _**z** i_ , the community assignments _ci_ are sampled from _p_ ( _ci|_ _**z** i_ ), which is parameterized by node and community embeddings. In order to generate an edge ( _i, j_ ), we sample another node embedding _**z** j_ from _p_ ( _**z**_ ) and respective community assignment _cj_ from _p_ ( _cj|_ _**z** j_ ). Afterwards, the node embeddings and the respective community assignments of node pairs are fed to a decoder. The decoder ensures that embeddings of both the nodes and the communities of connected nodes share high similarity. This enables learning such node embeddings that are useful for both community detection and node representation tasks.

We validate the effectiveness of our approach on several real-world graph datasets. In Sec. 4, we show empirically that VECODER is able to outperform the baseline methods including the direct competitors on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and nonoverlapping community detection. Furthermore, we compare the computational cost of training different algorithms. VECODER is up to 40x more time-efficient than its competitors. We also conduct hyperparameter sensitivity analysis which demonstrates the robustness of our approach. Our main contributions are summarized below:
- We propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for joint community detection and node representation learning.
- We adopt a novel approach and argue that a single node embedding is sufficient for learning both the representation of the node itself and its context.

- Training VECODER is extremely time-efficient in comparison to its competitors.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Community Detection.** Early community detection algorithms are inspired from clustering algorithms (Xie et al., 2013). For instance, spectral clustering (Tang & Liu, 2011) is applied to the graph Laplacian matrix for extracting the communities. Similarly, several matrix factorization based methods have been proposed to tackle the community detection problem. For example, Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)) treats the problem as a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) task. It aims to recover the node-community affiliation matrix and learns the latent factors which represent community affiliations of nodes. Another method CESNA(Yang et al. (2013)) extends Bigclam by modelling the interaction between the network structure and the node attributes. The performance of matrix factorization methods is limited due to the capacity of the bi-linear models. Some generative models, like vGraph (Sun et al., 2019), Circles (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012)) etc, have also been proposed to detect communities in a graph.

**Node Representation Learning.** Many successful algorithms which learn node representation in an unsupervised way are based on random walk objectives (Perozzi et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2015; Grover & Leskovec, 2016; Hamilton et al., 2017). Some known issues with random-walk based methods (e.g. DeepWalk, node2vec etc) are: (1) They sacrifice the structural information of the graph by putting over-emphasis on the proximity information (Ribeiro et al., 2017) and (2) great dependence of the performance on hyperparameters (walk-length, number of hops etc) (Perozzi et al., 2014; Grover & Leskovec, 2016). Recently, Gilmer et al. (2017) recently showed that graph convolutions encoder models greatly reduce the need for using the random-walk based training objectives. This is because the graph convolutions enforce that the neighboring nodes have similar representations. Some interesting GCN based approaches include graph autoencoders e.g. GAE and VGAE(Kipf & Welling (2016b)) and DGI(Velickovic et al., 2019).
**Joint community detection and node representation learning.** In the literature, several attempts have been made to tackle both these tasks in a single framework. Most of these methods propose

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

an alternate optimization process, i.e. learn node embeddings and improve community assignments with them and vice versa (Cavallari et al., 2017; Tu et al., 2018). Some approaches, like CNRL (Tu et al., 2018) and ComE (Cavallari et al., 2017), are inspired from random walk, thus inheriting the shortcomings of random walk. Others, like GEMSEC (Rozemberczki et al. (2019), are limited to the detection of non-overlapping communities. There also exist some generative models like CommunityGAN (Jia et al. (2019)) and vGraph (Sun et al. (2019)) that jointly learn community assignments and node embeddings. Some methods have high computational complexity, i.e. quadratic to the number of nodes in a graph, e.g. M-NMF (Wang et al. (2017)) and DNR (Yang et al., 2016a). CNRL, ComE and vGraph require learning two embeddings for each node for simultaneously tackling the two tasks. Unlike them, VECODER learns a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks.

It is pertinent to highlight that although both vGraph and VECODER adopt a variational approach but the underlying models are quite different. vGraph assumes that each node can be represented as a mixture of multiple communities and is described by a multinomial distribution over communities, whereas VECODER models the node embedding by a single distribution. For a given node, vGraph, first draws a community assignment and then a connected neighbor node is generated based on the assignment. Whereas, VECODER draws the node embedding from prior distribution and then community assignment is conditioned on a single node only. In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it. VECODER relies on the decoder to ensure that embeddings of the connected nodes and their communities share high similarity with each other.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

## 3.1 PROBLEM FORMULATION

Suppose an undirected graph _G_ = ( _V, E_ ) with the adjacency matrix _**A** ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ and a matrix _**X** ∈_ R _[N][×][F]_ of _F_ -dimensional node features, _N_ being the number of nodes. Given _K_ as the number of communities, we aim to jointly learn the node embeddings and the community embeddings following a variational approach such that: (1) One or more communities can be assigned to every node and (2) the node embeddings can be used for both community detection and node classification.

For better reconstructing the edges, Eq. (12) makes use of the community embeddings, node embeddings and community assignment information simultaneously. This helps in learning better node representations by leveraging the global information about the graph structure via community detection. On the other hand, this also forces the community assignment information to exploit the local graph structure via node embeddings and edge information.

## 3.4 PRACTICAL ASPECTS

The third term in Eq. (7) is estimated in practice using the samples generated by the approximate posterior. This term is equivalent to the negative of binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss between observed edges and reconstructed edges. Since community assignment follows a categorical distribution, we use Gumbel-softmax (Jang et al. (2016)) for backpropagation of the gradients. As for the second term of Eq. (7), it is also enough to set _M_ = 1, i.e. use only one sample per input node.

## 3.5 COMPLEXITY

Computation of dot products for all combinations of node and community embeddings takes _O_ ( _NKd_ ) time. Solving Eq. (11) further requires calculation of mean of dot products over the neighborhood for every node, which takes _O_ ( _|E|K_ ) computations overall as we traverse every edge for every community. Finally, we need softmax over all communities for every node in Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) which takes _O_ ( _NK_ ) time. Eq. (12) takes _O_ ( _|E|_ ) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products. As a result, the overall complexity becomes _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). This complexity is quite low compared to other algorithms designed to achieve similar goals (Cavallari et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016a).

We have selected 18 different datasets ranging from 270 to 126,842 edges. For nonoverlapping community detection and node classification, we use 5 the citation datasets (Bojchevski & G¨unnemann (2017); Yang et al. (2016b)). The remaining datasets (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012); Yang & Leskovec (2015)), used for overlapping community detection, are taken from SNAP repository (Leskovec & Krevl (2014)). Following (Sun et al., 2019), we take 5 biggest ground truth communities for youtube, amazon and dblp. Moreover, we also analyse the case of large number of communities. ## 4.2 BASELINES

For overlapping community detection, we compare with the following competitive baselines: **MNMF** (Wang et al., 2017) learns community membership distribution by using joint non-negative matrix factorization with modularity based regularization. **BIGCLAM** (Yang & Leskovec (2013)) also formulates community detection as a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) task. It simultaneously optimizes the model likelihood of observed links and learns the latent factors which represent community affiliations of nodes. **CESNA** (Yang et al. (2013)) extends BIGCLAM by statistically modelling the interaction between the network structure and the node attributes. **Circles** (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012)) introduces a generative model for community detection in ego-networks by learning node similarity metrics for every community. **SVI** (Gopalan & Blei (2013)) formulates membership of nodes in multiple communities by a Bayesian model of networks. **vGraph** (Sun et al. (2019)) simultaneously learns node embeddings and community assignments by modelling the nodes as being generated from a mixture of communities. **vGraph+** , a variant further incorporates regularization to weigh local connectivity. **ComE** (Cavallari et al. (2017)) jointly learns community and node embeddings by using gaussian mixture model formulation. **CNRL** (Tu et al., 2018) enhances the random walk sequences (generated by DeepWalk, node2vec etc) to jointly learn community and node embeddings. **CommunityGAN** (ComGAN)is a generative adversarial model for learning node embeddings such that the entries of the embedding vector of each node refer to the membership strength of the node to different communities. Lastly, we compare the results with the communities obtained by applying k-means to the learned embeddings of **DGI** (Velickovic et al., 2019).

For non-overlapping community detection and node classification, in addition to MNMF, DGI, CNRL, CommunityGAN, vGraph and ComE, we compare VECODER with the following baselines: **DeepWalk** (Perozzi et al. (2014)) makes use of SkipGram (Mikolov et al. (2013)) and truncated random walks on network to learn node embeddings. **LINE** (Tang et al. (2015)) learns node embeddings while attempting to preserve first and second order proximities of nodes. **Node2Vec** (Grover & Leskovec (2016)) learns the embeddings using biased random walk while aiming to preserve network neighborhoods of nodes. **Graph Autoencoder (GAE)** Kipf & Welling (2016b) extends the idea of autoencoders to graph datasets. We also include its variational counterpart i.e. **VGAE** . **GEMSEC** is a sequence sampling-based learning model which aims to jointly learn the node embeddings and clustering assignments.

## 4.3 SETTINGS

**For overlapping community detection** , we learn mean and log-variance matrices of 16dimensional node embeddings. We set _α_ = 0 _._ 9 and _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 in all our experiments. Following Kipf & Welling (2016b), we first pre-train a variational graph autoencoder. We perform gradient descent with Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba (2014)) and learning rate = 0 _._ 01. Community assignments are obtained using Eq. (14). For the baselines, we employ the results reported by Sun et al. (2019). For evaluating the performance, we use _F1-score_ and _Jaccard similarity_ .

**For non-overlapping community detection** , since the default implementations of most the baselines use 128 dimensional embeddings, for we use _d_ = 128 for fair comparison. Eq. (13) is used for community assignments. For vGraph, we use the code provided by the authors. We employ _normalized mutual information (NMI)_ and _adjusted random index (ARI)_ as evaluation metrics.

**For node classification** , we follow the training split used in various previous works (Yang et al., 2016b; Kipf & Welling, 2016a; Velickovic et al., 2019), i.e. 20 nodes per class for training. We train logistic regression using LIBLINEAR (Fan et al. (2008)) solver as our classifier and report the evaluation results on rest of the nodes. For the algorithms that do not use node features, we train the classifier by appending the raw node features with the learnt embeddings. For evaluation, we use _F1-macro_ and _F1-micro_ scores.
All the reported results are the average over five runs. Further implementation details can be found in the code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/1d95bf8f-8ce3-4870-a454-07db463b419f.

## 4.4 DISCUSSION OF RESULTS

In the following, we discuss the results to gain some important insights into the problem.

Tables 2 and 3 summarize the results of the performance comparison for the overlapping community detection task.

First, we note that our proposed method VECODER outperforms the competitive methods on all datasets in terms of Jaccard Similarity. VECODER also outperforms its competitors on 12 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. It is the second best method on the 13th dataset ( _fb0_ ). These results demonstrate the capability of VECODER to learn multiple community assignments quite well and hence reinforces our intuition behind the design of Eq. (11).

Second, we observe that there is no consistent performing algorithm among the competitive methods. That is, excluding VECODER , the best performance is achieved by vGraph/vGraph+ on 5, ComGAN on 4 and ComE on 3 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. A a similar trend can be seen in Jaccard Similarity. Third, it is noted that all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. This supports our claim that treating the two tasks jointly results in better performance.

Fourth, we observe that vGraph+ results are generally better than vGraph. This is because vGraph+ incorporates a regularization term in the loss function which is based on Jaccard coefficients of connected nodes as edge weights. However, it should be noted that this prepossessing step is computationally expensive for densely connected graphs.

Tab. 4 shows the results on non-overlapping community detection. First, we observe that MNMF, DeepWalk, LINE and Node2Vec provide a good baseline for the task. However, these methods are not able to achieve comparable performance on any dataset relative to the frameworks that treat the two tasks jointly. Second, VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitors in NMI and ARI metrics, except for _CiteSeer_ where it achieves second best ARI. Third, we observe that GCN based models i.e. GAE, VGAE and DGI show competitive performance. That is, they achieve second best performance in all the datasets except _CiteSeer_ . In particular, DGI achieves second best NMI results in 3 out of 5 datasets and 2 out of 5 datsets in terms of ARI. Nonetheless, DGI results are not very competitive in Tab. 2 and Tab. 3, showing that while DGI can be a good choice for learning node embeddings for attributed graphs with non-overlapping communities, it is not the best option for non-attributed graphs or overlapping communities.

The results for node classification are presented in Tab. 5. VECODER achieves best F1-micro and F1-macro scores on 4 out of 5 datasets. We also observe that GCN based models i.e. GAE, VGAE and DGI show competitive performance, following the trend in results of Tab. 4. Furthermore, we note that the node classification results of CommunityGan (ComGAN) are quite poor. We think a potential reason behind it is that the node embeddings are constrained to have same dimensions as the number of communities. Hence, different components of the learned node embeddings simply represent the membership strengths of nodes for different communities. The linear classifiers may find it difficult to separate such vectors.

## 4.5 HYPERPARAMETER SENSITIVITY

We study the dependence of VECODER on _ϵ_ and _α_ by evaluating on four datasets of different sizes: _fb698_ ( _N_ = 61), _fb1912_ ( _N_ = 747), _amazon1000_ (N=1540) and _youtube_ ( _N_ = 5346). We sweep for _ϵ_ = _{_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 2 _, · · · ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ . For demonstrating effect of _α_ , we fix _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 and sweep for _α_ = _{_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 2 _, · · · ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ . The average results of five runs for _ϵ_ and _α_ are given in Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b respectively. Overall VECODER is quite robust to the change in the values of _ϵ_ and _α_ . In case of _ϵ_ , we see a general trend of decrease in performance when the threshold _ϵ_ is set quite high e.g. _ϵ >_ 0 _._ 7. This is because the datasets contain overlapping communities and a very high _ϵ_ will cause the algorithm to give only the most probable community assignment instead of potentially providing multiple communities per node. However, for a large part of sweep space, the results are almost consistent. When _ϵ_ is fixed and _α_ is changed, the results are mostly consistent except when _α_ is set to a low value. Eq. (11) shows that in such a case the node itself is almost neglected and VECODER tends to assign communities based upon neighborhood only, which may cause a decrease in the performance. This effect is most visible in _amazon1000_ dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities. Therefore, only depending upon the neighbors will most likely result in poor results.

## 4.6 TRAINING TIME

Now we compare the training times of different algorithms in Fig. 2. As some of the baselines are more resource intensive than others, we select aws instance type g4dn.4xlarge for fair comparison of training times. For vGraph, we train for 1000 iterations and for VECODER for 1500 iterations. For all other algorithms we use the default parameters as used in section 4.3. We observe that the methods that simply output the node embeddings take relatively less time compared to the algorithms that jointly learn node representations and community assignments e.g VECODER , vGraph and CNRL. Among these algorithms VECODER is the most time efficient. It consistently trains in less time compared to its direct competitors. For instance, it is about 12 times faster than ComE for _CiteSeer-full_ and about 40 times faster compared to vGraph for _Cora-full_ dataset. This provides evidence for lower computational complexity of VECODER in Section 3.5.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We propose a scalable generative method VECODER to simultaneously perform community detection and node representation learning. Our novel approach learns a single community-aware node embedding for both the representation of the node and its context. VECODER is scalable due to its low complexity, i.e. _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). The experiments on several graph datasets show that VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitive baselines on node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection tasks. Moreover, training the VECODER is highly time-efficient than its competitors.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper addresses a genuinely interesting and well-motivated problem—jointly learning node embeddings and community assignments within a single generative framework—and the central claim that a *single* node embedding can suffice (as opposed to the dual node/context embeddings used by CNRL, ComE, and vGraph) is a reasonable and potentially valuable contribution. However, based on the introduction and related work, the paper appears to have several significant weaknesses that would concern a reviewer. First, the technical novelty seems incremental: the generative story described (sample **z** ~ p(**z**), then c ~ p(c|**z**), then decode edges) is closely related to existing VAE-style graph models (e.g., VGAE and vGraph), yet the introduction does not clearly articulate what is methodologically new beyond collapsing two embeddings into one, nor does it justify theoretically why a single embedding should be sufficient. Second, the writing contains notable clarity and polish issues—including a broken/garbled title ("VECODER VARIATIONAL EMBEDDINGS"), awkward phrasing, and an acronym ("VECODER" from "Variational Embeddings for Community Detection and node Representation") whose derivation is strained—which suggests the manuscript needs careful revision. Third, the claimed contributions are largely empirical (outperforming baselines on three tasks, 40× speedup, hyperparameter robustness), but the introduction gives no intuition for *why* the method should be faster or more accurate, making the contributions feel more like engineering results than scientific insights. Finally, key design choices—such as the form of the prior p(**z**), the decoder architecture, and how community and node embeddings interact—are only vaguely sketched, so the reader cannot yet assess whether the improvements stem from a principled modeling advance or from incidental architectural tuning. Strengthening the theoretical motivation, sharpening the distinction from vGraph in particular, and substantially improving the writing would be necessary for this paper to be competitive.

comments.title: Factorization of q_φ(z_i, z_j, c_i, c_j | I) relies on unstated independence assumptions
comments.quote: The distribution _qφ_ ( _**z** i,_ _**z** j, ci, cj|I_ ) in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions i.e.
comments.explanation: The passage says the joint posterior is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions, but (a) the actual factorization equation is not visible in the passage shown (likely an OCR gap), and (b) the justification is not given. For the generative model stated earlier, z_i and z_j are a priori independent and c_i only depends on z_i, but in the posterior conditioned on I (which includes A, hence edge structure), z_i and z_j are generally not independent. So the 'conditional independence' factorization is a variational approximation choice, not a consequence of the model. The text treats it as if it follows, which could mislead a careful reader into thinking this is exact. It should be labeled as a mean-field variational assumption.

comments.title: Claim that VECODER does not need edge information in the generative process
comments.quote: In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it.
comments.explanation: At first reading this seems like a clean contrast with vGraph. But looking at Section 3.2, the generative model explicitly includes p_θ(a_ij | c_i, c_j, z_i, z_j) (the edge decoder) as part of the joint distribution, and edges a_ij are precisely what is generated. So VECODER absolutely uses edge information in its generative process — edges are the observed variables being modeled. What the authors probably mean is that the sampling of c_i does not condition on edges (unlike vGraph, where community assignments are drawn per edge). The current wording is misleading: taken literally it contradicts Eq. (5) and the whole point of the edge decoder. This should be rephrased to say that community assignments are not conditioned on edges, rather than that edge information is absent from the generative process.

comments.title: Amazon subsets description inconsistency
comments.quote:
we prepare two subsets

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

of amazon dataset by randomly selecting 500 and 1000 communities from 2000 smallest communities in the amazon dataset.
comments.explanation: The authors describe preparing amazon500 and amazon1000 by selecting 500 and 1000 communities from the 2000 smallest communities in the amazon dataset. However, the main amazon entry in Table 1 has K=5 (following Sun et al. 2019, using the 5 biggest communities). So the 'amazon dataset' referenced here for subset preparation is presumably the raw full amazon dataset (not the K=5 version used in the table). This is ambiguous — a reader could reasonably wonder how amazon500/amazon1000 relate to the amazon row in Table 1, since the latter has only 5 communities. The description is underspecified: which base amazon graph (full vs. the 5-community version) is used, and are the 500/1000 communities subgraphs induced by the selected communities? The |V| and |E| counts in Table 1 for amazon500 and amazon1000 suggest induced subgraphs, but this is not explicitly stated.

comments.title: Complexity analysis omits encoder cost
comments.quote: As a result, the overall complexity becomes _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). This complexity is quite low compared to other algorithms designed to achieve similar goals
comments.explanation: The complexity derivation accounts for the community-assignment posterior (Eq. 11), the softmax normalizations (Eq. 10, 11), and the edge decoder (Eq. 12). However, it does not include the cost of the encoder q_φ(z_i|I), which in Section 3.3 is described as potentially being a GCN, GAT, or GraphSAGE. These typically cost O(|E|d + Nd^2) per layer. The claim 'overall complexity becomes O(|E|K + NKd)' is therefore incomplete — it is the complexity of the community/edge components conditional on embeddings, not the full training complexity. For fair comparison with baselines (which include encoder costs), this omission could mislead about the true comparative complexity.

comments.title: Inconsistency between claim of consistently outperforming all competitors and exception noted
comments.quote: VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitive baselines on node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection tasks.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states VECODER consistently outperforms ALL baselines on all three tasks. However, in the results discussion, the authors themselves note: 'VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitors in NMI and ARI metrics, except for CiteSeer where it achieves second best ARI' and 'VECODER achieves best F1-micro and F1-macro scores on 4 out of 5 datasets' for node classification. The paper summary also notes 'VECODER outperforms competitors on all 13 overlapping datasets in Jaccard, and 12/13 in F1 (second on fb0).' So there are documented exceptions on all three tasks, but the conclusion overstates by saying 'consistently outperforms all.' This is a mild overclaim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in node classification setup for methods using features
comments.quote: For the algorithms that do not use node features, we train the classifier by appending the raw node features with the learnt embeddings.
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this means only the baselines that don't use features get this concatenation, while VECODER (which uses features via the encoder) does not. That would give a fair comparison. However, this creates some ambiguity: if baselines have features concatenated to their embeddings while VECODER only uses its 128-dim learned embeddings, it's unclear whether this favors either side. A careful reader might find the protocol insufficiently specified for reproducibility — in particular, whether VECODER's classifier input is just z_i or z_i concatenated with X_i. This is a minor reproducibility concern for the node classification comparison.

comments.title: i.i.d. assumption for c_i | z_i is oddly stated
comments.quote: Furthermore, assuming _ci|_ _**z** i_ to be _i.i.d_ random variables, the joint distributions in (2) can be factorized as
comments.explanation: Initially I was puzzled: c_i | z_i being 'i.i.d.' is a strange phrasing, because i.i.d. normally applies to a collection of variables with the same marginal distribution. Here the authors seem to mean that given the {z_i}, the community assignments {c_i} are conditionally independent (and presumably identically distributed in the sense that each c_i depends only on its own z_i via the same mechanism p_θ(c_i|z_i)). The intent is reasonable and standard for this kind of latent variable model, but the phrasing is imprecise — strictly, c_i|z_i and c_j|z_j won't be identically distributed unless z_i = z_j. A careful reader can infer 'conditionally independent given Z', but the statement as written is technically sloppy.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—jointly learning node representations and community assignments—and the core motivation for a unified, efficient model is clear and potentially valuable, especially if the claimed empirical gains and computational savings over CNRL, ComE, and vGraph are substantiated. The introduction is generally readable and positions the work reasonably well, but the technical novelty is not yet fully convincing from the opening: the claim that a single embedding suffices for both node representation and context/community modeling needs sharper theoretical or empirical justification, since many embedding methods use separate “source/context” embeddings for optimization rather than as an inherently wasteful modeling choice. The generative story is also described at a high level but remains somewhat vague: it is unclear how communities are represented, how overlapping assignments are parameterized, how the decoder combines node and community similarity, and what variational inference objective is optimized. The related work discussion is relevant but could be more critical and precise, especially in distinguishing VECODER from prior generative and variational graph embedding/community models. The writing is mostly clear, though there are formatting and grammatical issues, occasional awkward phrasing, and some overstatement in claims such as “extremely time-efficient” and “outperforms … on all three tasks” before the evidence is presented. The most significant issues to watch for are whether the model’s novelty over existing joint embedding/community approaches is substantial, whether the evaluation is fair and sufficiently broad, whether hyperparameters and community counts are handled realistically, and whether the paper explains why the proposed variational formulation is necessary rather than simply an efficient objective for combining proximity and community regularization.

comments.title: Categorical latent community variable is inconsistent with claimed overlapping memberships
comments.quote: Given K as the number of communities, we aim to jointly learn the node embeddings and the community embeddings following a variational approach such that: (1) One or more communities can be assigned to every node ... Since community assignment follows a categorical distribution, we use Gumbel-softmax ...
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly describes VECODER as supporting nodes that belong to one or more communities, but the formal latent variable is a single categorical assignment modeled by a softmax and relaxed with Gumbel-softmax. A categorical distribution represents one mutually exclusive community draw, not a multi-hot set of memberships or independent affiliation indicators. Thresholding the softmax probability vector can produce multiple reported labels after training, but that is a post-processing heuristic rather than a generative model of overlapping membership. The simplex constraint also limits what thresholding can produce: with epsilon = 0.3, at most three communities can be selected, and for high thresholds the rule can select no community unless an argmax fallback is specified. The claims should either be weakened to say overlap is inferred from thresholded soft probabilities, or the model should use a genuine multilabel/mixed-membership latent formulation.

comments.title: Unsupported blanket claim that BigCLAM performs poorly for node-representation tasks
comments.quote: Looking from the other perspective, methods like Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)), that focus on finding the community structure in the dataset, perform poorly for node-representation tasks e.g. node classification.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is understandable: BigCLAM is optimized for community affiliation and link structure rather than general-purpose node embeddings. However, the sentence states an empirical performance claim. BigCLAM produces node-community affiliation features that could in principle be used for classification, and the paper does not appear to include BigCLAM among the node-classification baselines or cite evidence for this specific claim. A more defensible wording would say that BigCLAM is not designed primarily for node-representation tasks.

comments.title: Neighborhood-dependent posterior appears inconsistent with the stated conditional factorization
comments.quote: The corresponding approximate posterior qφ (ci = k| zi, I ) in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding zi as well as the neighborhood.
comments.explanation: The idea of using neighborhood information to infer a node's community is reasonable, but it is unclear how this matches the variational factorization. If Eq. (11) uses sampled neighbor embeddings z_j for j in N_i, then q(c_i | z_i, I) depends on other latent variables, coupling variational factors across neighboring nodes. That would need to be reflected in the ELBO and posterior factorization. If the authors instead use deterministic encoder means or an observed-data-derived neighborhood representation, that should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Claim that VECODER does not require edge information in the generative process conflicts with the edge decoder
comments.quote: In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it. VECODER relies on the decoder to ensure that embeddings of the connected nodes and their communities share high similarity with each other.
comments.explanation: The likely intended distinction is that VECODER's priors over node embeddings and community assignments do not condition on observed neighborhoods. However, the model explicitly includes an edge decoder pθ(aij | ci,cj,zi,zj) and is trained to model the adjacency matrix. In that standard sense, edge variables are part of the generative model. The statement should be clarified to say that edge information enters through the decoder/likelihood rather than through the prior.

comments.title: The edge-community intuition overstates what sparse community graphs imply
comments.quote: Finally, the choice of edge decoder in Eq. (5) is motivated by the intuition that the nodes connected by edges have a high probability of belonging to the same community and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The first direction is standard for assortative community detection: adjacent nodes are more likely to share a community. The 'vice versa' is too strong for sparse graphs. Sharing a community usually raises the probability of an edge relative to background, but same-community nodes need not have a high absolute probability of being connected. The decoder may still be reasonable, but the prose risks implying clique-like communities rather than sparse assortative structure.

comments.title: Random-walk framing of cited node representation methods is imprecise
comments.quote: Many successful algorithms which learn node representation in an unsupervised way are based on random walk objectives (Perozzi et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2015; Grover & Leskovec, 2016; Hamilton et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: DeepWalk and node2vec are random-walk-based, but the cited list also includes methods whose objectives are not straightforwardly random-walk objectives. LINE is primarily formulated around first- and second-order proximity through edge-based objectives, and GraphSAGE is an inductive neighborhood aggregation framework, even though its unsupervised loss may use random-walk-derived positive pairs. The sentence should distinguish random-walk methods from proximity-preserving and neighborhood-aggregation methods.

comments.title: Reconstruction objective and complexity analysis are unclear about non-edges, negative sampling, and pairwise decoder costs
comments.quote: This term is equivalent to the negative of binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss between observed edges and reconstructed edges. ... Eq. (12) takes O(|E|) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products.
comments.explanation: If the edge decoder defines a Bernoulli likelihood over the adjacency matrix, the BCE reconstruction term normally includes both edges and non-edges, or uses an explicit negative-sampling or sparse weighted-BCE approximation. The text refers only to observed edges and the complexity analysis counts only |E| edge computations, which appears inconsistent with the earlier full-adjacency likelihood unless a sampling approximation is being used. In addition, if the decoder uses node-node terms such as z_i^T z_j, computing it over observed edges costs at least O(|E|d), not O(|E|), unless an implementation trick is specified. The paper should state whether reconstruction is over all N^2 pairs, a sparse weighted objective, or sampled negatives, and update the runtime accordingly.

comments.title: Construction of Amazon subsets and reuse of baseline results are underspecified
comments.quote: we prepare two subsets of amazon dataset by randomly selecting 500 and 1000 communities from 2000 smallest communities in the amazon dataset. ... For the baselines, we employ the results reported by Sun et al. (2019).
comments.explanation: The Amazon500 and Amazon1000 subset construction is not reproducible from the description. It is unclear whether the node set is the union of selected communities, whether induced subgraphs are used, how edges to outside nodes are handled, how labels outside the selected communities are treated, and what sampling seed or protocol produced the reported graph statistics. This also makes the reuse of baseline results ambiguous: Sun et al. may not have reported results on these exact random subsets with the same preprocessing. The paper should clarify which baselines were copied, which were rerun, and whether identical preprocessing and evaluation scripts were used.

comments.title: Key training details for VECODER are not fully specified
comments.quote: Following Kipf & Welling (2016b), we first pre-train a variational graph autoencoder. We perform gradient descent with Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba (2014)) and learning rate = 0.01.
comments.explanation: The paper gives some hyperparameters, but important details needed for reproduction remain implicit: the encoder architecture used in each experiment, the number of pretraining and VECODER training epochs, Gumbel-softmax temperature or annealing schedule, negative-sampling or reconstruction details, dropout/weight decay, and whether the same alpha and other hyperparameters are used across overlapping detection, non-overlapping detection, and node classification. A code link is helpful, but the main experimental choices should be specified in the paper.

comments.title: Overlapping-community F1 and Jaccard metrics are not sufficiently specified
comments.quote: Table 2: F1 scores for overlapping communities. Best and second best values are bold and blue respectively.
comments.explanation: For overlapping community detection, F1 and Jaccard are not uniquely defined. They may be computed using pairwise node co-membership, per-community matching, bipartite alignment, micro/macro averaging, or SNAP-style ground-truth matching. These choices can materially change the reported numbers. The paper names the metrics but does not define the exact matching and averaging protocol, leaving a reproducibility gap for the central empirical comparison.

comments.title: Discussion of joint methods overgeneralizes and misclassifies baselines
comments.quote: all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. ... MNMF, DeepWalk, LINE and Node2Vec ... are not able to achieve comparable performance ... relative to the frameworks that treat the two tasks jointly.
comments.explanation: The paper's qualitative claim that joint embedding/community methods often perform well is plausible, but the universal statements are not accurate. In Table 3, fb348 appears to have DGI as the best non-VECODER Jaccard result, while DGI is described as an embedding method followed by clustering rather than a joint community-detection framework. Separately, MNMF/M-NMF is itself generally presented as a joint network embedding and community-detection method, and the paper's baseline description emphasizes its community membership distributions and modularity regularization. Grouping MNMF with purely embedding baselines and contrasting it against joint frameworks is therefore misleading.

comments.title: Count of best competing F1 methods omits one dataset
comments.quote: That is, excluding VECODER, the best performance is achieved by vGraph/vGraph+ on 5, ComGAN on 4 and ComE on 3 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score.
comments.explanation: The listed counts sum to only 12 of the 13 datasets. The missing case appears to be Amazon, where CNRL has the best non-VECODER F1 score at 53.5, slightly above vGraph and vGraph+. The broader point that no single competitor dominates may still hold, but the enumeration is incomplete.

comments.title: Empirical outperforming claims lack uncertainty despite close margins
comments.quote: First, we note that our proposed method VECODER outperforms the competitive methods on all datasets in terms of Jaccard Similarity.
comments.explanation: The statement is correct as a comparison of the reported table means, but the paper says results are averaged over five runs while the table gives no standard deviations, confidence intervals, or significance tests. Some margins are small, such as fb0 and fb348. Reporting variability would better support the strength of the outperforming claim, especially where random initialization, sampling, and splits can introduce nontrivial variance.

comments.title: Average community size for amazon1000 is incorrectly inferred as N/K despite overlapping communities
comments.quote: This effect is most visible in amazon1000 dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities.
comments.explanation: The number 1.54 appears to come from N/K = 1540/1000. For an overlapping-community dataset, however, average community size should be the total number of node-community memberships divided by K, not the number of distinct nodes divided by K. Because nodes may belong to multiple selected Amazon communities, N/K does not establish that communities have only 1.54 members on average. The explanation should use the actual post-preprocessing ground-truth community sizes or be phrased more cautiously.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—jointly learning node representations and community assignments—and the core motivation for a unified, efficient model is clear and potentially valuable, especially if the claimed empirical gains and computational savings over CNRL, ComE, and vGraph are substantiated. The introduction is generally readable and positions the work reasonably well, but the technical novelty is not yet fully convincing from the opening: the claim that a single embedding suffices for both node representation and context/community modeling needs sharper theoretical or empirical justification, since many embedding methods use separate “source/context” embeddings for optimization rather than as an inherently wasteful modeling choice. The generative story is also described at a high level but remains somewhat vague: it is unclear how communities are represented, how overlapping assignments are parameterized, how the decoder combines node and community similarity, and what variational inference objective is optimized. The related work discussion is relevant but could be more critical and precise, especially in distinguishing VECODER from prior generative and variational graph embedding/community models. The writing is mostly clear, though there are formatting and grammatical issues, occasional awkward phrasing, and some overstatement in claims such as “extremely time-efficient” and “outperforms … on all three tasks” before the evidence is presented. The most significant issues to watch for are whether the model’s novelty over existing joint embedding/community approaches is substantial, whether the evaluation is fair and sufficiently broad, whether hyperparameters and community counts are handled realistically, and whether the paper explains why the proposed variational formulation is necessary rather than simply an efficient objective for combining proximity and community regularization.

comments.title: Claimed overlapping memberships are not matched by the stated single categorical latent assignment
comments.quote: VECODER assumes that every node can be a member of one or more communities.
comments.explanation: At first this reads as the standard overlapping-community assumption, and it is central to the paper’s positioning. I checked the later formalization to see whether the model uses a multi-label membership vector, independent Bernoulli community indicators, or mixed-membership proportions. Instead, the methodology introduces a single latent variable \(c_i\) per node, models \(p_\theta(c_i\mid \mathbf z_i)\) with a softmax over communities, and uses Gumbel-softmax for a categorical community assignment. The later overlapping inference rule thresholds a probability vector, which can yield multiple reported communities, but that is not the same as the generative model assuming that the node actually has multiple simultaneous community memberships. A categorical latent variable encodes one community draw, not a set of memberships, unless the authors explicitly reinterpret the softmax probabilities as affiliation strengths rather than as a categorical distribution. The prose would be accurate if weakened to say that overlapping communities are inferred from soft community probabilities, or the model would need a multi-label latent variable to support the stated assumption directly.

comments.title: Unsupported blanket claim that BigCLAM performs poorly for node-representation tasks
comments.quote: Looking from the other perspective, methods like Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)), that focus on finding the community structure in the dataset, perform poorly for node-representation tasks e.g. node classification.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: BigCLAM is optimized for community affiliation/link structure rather than general-purpose node embeddings, so it may be less suitable for downstream representation tasks. However, the sentence states an empirical performance claim rather than a design limitation. I checked the later experimental setup, and BigCLAM appears among overlapping-community baselines but not among the node-classification baselines, so the paper context shown does not directly substantiate this claim. Since BigCLAM does produce node-community affiliation features that could in principle be used for classification, a careful reader may reasonably expect either a citation, an experiment, or a more qualified statement such as “are not designed primarily for node-representation tasks.”

comments.title: Categorical community variable does not naturally support overlapping memberships
comments.quote: Given _K_ as the number of communities, we aim to jointly learn the node embeddings and the community embeddings following a variational approach such that: (1) One or more communities can be assigned to every node and (2) the node embeddings can be used for both community detection and node classification.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a commitment that the probabilistic model itself supports multi-membership communities. Looking ahead in the same section, however, the community assignment variable is described through a softmax over communities, and the practical section says the assignment follows a categorical distribution with Gumbel-softmax. A categorical latent variable represents one community label per draw, not a subset of communities. The later thresholding of a softmax probability vector can produce multiple reported labels, but that is a post-processing heuristic rather than a generative model of overlapping membership, and it is not equivalent to using independent Bernoulli memberships or a multi-hot latent vector. The authors are right that soft probabilities can be thresholded to obtain overlapping outputs, but the statement that 'one or more communities can be assigned to every node' is stronger than what the formal latent-variable model appears to encode.

comments.title: Neighborhood-dependent posterior appears inconsistent with the stated conditional factorization
comments.quote: The corresponding approximate posterior _qφ_ ( _ci_ = _k|_ _**z** i, I_ ) in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding _**z** i_ as well as the neighborhood. To design this, our intuition is to consider the similarity of _**g** k_ with the embedding _**z** i_ as well as with the embeddings of the neighbors of the _i_ -th node.
comments.explanation: The intended idea is clear and sensible: use both the node's own embedding and neighborhood information to infer its community. What remains technically unclear is how this is reconciled with the posterior notation and factorization just above, where the assignment posterior is written as depending on only _z_i_ and observed input _I_. If Eq. (11) actually uses sampled neighbor embeddings _z_j_ for _j \in N_i_, then _q(c_i \mid z_i,I)_ is not only a function of _z_i_ and observed data; it also depends on other latent variables. That would couple the variational factors across neighboring nodes and would invalidate a fully factorized derivation unless the ELBO accounts for that dependence. If, instead, the authors intend to use deterministic encoder means or some fixed function of _I_ for neighbors, that should be stated explicitly. As written, a careful reader cannot tell whether the posterior factorization is mathematically valid or whether Eq. (11) introduces additional latent-variable dependencies omitted from the notation.

comments.title: Claim that VECODER does not require edge information in the generative process conflicts with the edge decoder
comments.quote: In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it. VECODER relies on the decoder to ensure that embeddings of the connected nodes and their communities share high similarity with each other.
comments.explanation: I understand the likely intended contrast: vGraph may condition the generation of a neighboring node on a sampled community, whereas VECODER first samples node embeddings and community assignments independently and then uses a decoder over pairs. But the wording is misleading because VECODER's generative model explicitly includes an edge decoder _p_θ(_a_{ij} \mid c_i,c_j,z_i,z_j_) and is trained to model the adjacency matrix. In that standard sense, edge variables are part of the generative model. The sentence could be made correct by saying that VECODER's prior over node embeddings and community assignments does not condition on observed edge neighborhoods, or that edge information enters only through the decoder/likelihood. As written, it seems to deny the role of edge generation that is introduced immediately afterward.

comments.title: The edge-community intuition is stronger than what sparse community graphs justify
comments.quote: Finally, the choice of edge decoder in Eq. (5) is motivated by the intuition that the nodes connected by edges have a high probability of belonging to the same community and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The first half of the intuition is standard for assortative community detection: adjacent nodes are more likely to share a community. The 'vice versa' is more problematic. In most real sparse graphs, sharing a community only increases the probability of an edge; it does not imply that two same-community nodes have a high absolute probability of being connected. Communities are dense relative to the background, not cliques. If the decoder merely gives higher probability to edges for similar/community-sharing nodes, the model is reasonable, but the prose overstates the assumption and may mislead readers about what kind of community structure the method can model, especially for large sparse graphs or overlapping communities.

comments.title: Random-walk framing of cited node representation methods is imprecise
comments.quote: Many successful algorithms which learn node representation in an unsupervised way are based on random walk objectives (Perozzi et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2015; Grover & Leskovec, 2016; Hamilton et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The general point that DeepWalk and node2vec rely on random-walk context generation is correct. However, the citation list mixes in methods whose objectives are not straightforwardly random-walk based. LINE (Tang et al., 2015) is primarily formulated around preserving first- and second-order proximity through edge-based objectives, and GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017) is an inductive neighborhood aggregation framework, even though its unsupervised loss can use random-walk-derived positive pairs. This does not affect the proposed method, but the sentence overgeneralizes the cited literature. A more precise phrasing would distinguish random-walk methods from proximity-preserving and neighborhood-aggregation methods.

comments.title: Reconstruction/BCE objective is unclear about non-edges and sampling
comments.quote: The third term in Eq. (7) is estimated in practice using the samples generated by the approximate posterior. This term is equivalent to the negative of binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss between observed edges and reconstructed edges.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended connection: if the edge decoder is a Bernoulli likelihood for adjacency entries, then the expected log-likelihood term in the ELBO can be implemented as a negative BCE reconstruction loss, as in VGAE-style models. However, the wording says BCE is between “observed edges” and reconstructed edges, which could mean only positive edges. A Bernoulli adjacency likelihood/BCE normally includes both edges and non-edges, or uses an explicitly specified negative-sampling or class-weighting approximation. This matters because using only positive edges would not correspond to the stated likelihood over the adjacency matrix and could lead to a degenerate reconstruction objective. The surrounding complexity discussion later counts only \(|E|\) edge computations, which reinforces the ambiguity rather than resolving it. The paper should specify whether BCE is over all \(N^2\) adjacency entries, a sparse weighted BCE, or a negative-sampling approximation.

comments.title: Categorical community latent variable conflicts with overlapping-community inference
comments.quote: Since community assignment follows a categorical distribution, we use Gumbel-softmax (Jang et al. (2016)) for backpropagation of the gradients.
comments.explanation: The use of Gumbel-softmax is standard for differentiating through a categorical latent assignment, so that part is technically sensible for a single-label community variable. The concern is that the paper repeatedly claims that each node may belong to one or more communities, and immediately afterward proposes overlapping assignments by thresholding a probability vector. A categorical distribution, even with a Gumbel-softmax relaxation, represents one mutually exclusive community draw; its probabilities lie on a simplex and compete with each other. Thresholding the resulting softmax probabilities can produce multiple labels post hoc, but it does not make the generative model a true multi-label or mixed-membership model. This is especially consequential because the maximum number of labels above a threshold \(\epsilon\) is constrained by the simplex, and with the reported \(\epsilon=0.3\), at most three communities can be assigned to any node. Thus the model’s formal latent variable does not fully match the stated overlapping-community assumption.

comments.title: Complexity of the edge decoder appears to omit node-pair costs and non-edge reconstruction
comments.quote: Eq. (12) takes _O_ ( _|E|_ ) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products. As a result, the overall complexity becomes _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ).
comments.explanation: The preceding dot products are between node embeddings and community embeddings, and those do justify the \(O(NKd)\) term. But the decoder is described earlier as using node embeddings, community embeddings, and community assignment information jointly, and the model factorization is over adjacency entries \(a_{ij}\). If Eq. (12) includes node-node similarities such as \(z_i^\top z_j\), then computing the decoder over observed edges costs at least \(O(|E|d)\), not \(O(|E|)\), unless an additional implementation trick is being used. If the likelihood/BCE is over all adjacency entries, the cost is closer to \(O(N^2)\) or requires negative sampling, neither of which is reflected here. The claim that all required dot products have already been calculated is therefore not justified by the dot products described just above.

comments.title: The complexity analysis does not match the stated full-adjacency likelihood
comments.quote: Finally, we need softmax over all communities for every node in Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) which takes _O_ ( _NK_ ) time. Eq. (12) takes _O_ ( _|E|_ ) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products.
comments.explanation: Earlier, the generative model factorizes \(p_\theta(\mathbf A\mid \mathbf Z,\mathbf c)\) over pairs \((i,j)\), which conventionally includes both edges and non-edges in the adjacency matrix. Here, however, the runtime is counted only over \(|E|\) observed edges. That can be a valid sparse training approximation, but only if the authors explicitly state that they optimize the reconstruction term using observed edges plus some negative-sampling or sparse-BCE scheme. Without that clarification, the complexity result seems to analyze a different objective from the one formally defined in the ELBO.

comments.title: Construction of the large-community Amazon subsets is underspecified
comments.quote:
Moreover, we also analyse the case of large number of communities. For this purpose, we prepare two subsets

of amazon dataset by randomly selecting 500 and 1000 communities from 2000 smallest communities in the amazon dataset.
comments.explanation: The goal of testing scalability with many communities is clear and useful. However, the subset construction is not reproducible from this description. A reader cannot tell whether the node set is the union of nodes belonging to the sampled communities, whether edges to nodes outside the selected communities are removed, whether induced subgraphs are used, how overlapping labels outside the sampled set are handled, or what random seed/sampling protocol produced the reported \(|V|\) and \(|E|\). Because these subsets are part of the empirical evidence for handling large \(K\), these preprocessing details are methodologically important rather than cosmetic.

comments.title: Use of previously reported baseline results is ambiguous for newly constructed datasets
comments.quote: For the baselines, we employ the results reported by Sun et al. (2019).
comments.explanation: Reusing published baseline results can be reasonable when the datasets, preprocessing, community counts, and evaluation protocol exactly match the cited work. But the passage also introduces newly prepared Amazon500 and Amazon1000 subsets, and it is not clear that Sun et al. (2019) reported results on these exact random subsets. If some baseline numbers are imported while others are newly run, or if preprocessing differs, the comparison may not be apples-to-apples. The paper should clarify which baseline results were copied, which were rerun, and whether the same graph preprocessing and evaluation scripts were used.

comments.title: Key training details for VECODER are not fully specified in the settings
comments.quote: Following Kipf & Welling (2016b), we first pre-train a variational graph autoencoder. We perform gradient descent with Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba (2014)) and learning rate = 0 _._ 01.
comments.explanation: The passage gives some useful hyperparameters, such as embedding dimension, \(\alpha\), \(\epsilon\), optimizer, and learning rate for overlapping community detection. But several choices needed to reproduce the method remain implicit: the encoder architecture used in each experiment, number of pretraining and VECODER training epochs, Gumbel-softmax temperature/annealing schedule, whether and how negative samples are drawn for reconstruction, weight decay/dropout, and whether \(\alpha\) or other hyperparameters are reused for non-overlapping detection and node classification. A code link helps, but the paper itself should state the main experimental choices, especially because the method allows multiple encoder networks and relies on stochastic relaxations.

comments.title: Categorical softmax posterior is only an ad hoc basis for overlapping memberships
comments.quote: These results demonstrate the capability of VECODER to learn multiple community assignments quite well and hence reinforces our intuition behind the design of Eq. (11).
comments.explanation: I understand the intended argument: Tables 2 and 3 show strong empirical performance on overlapping-community benchmarks, and Eq. (11) produces a full vector of community probabilities that can be thresholded to obtain more than one label. However, checking this against the model definition leaves a substantive concern. Earlier, \(c_i\) is modeled as a single categorical latent variable with a softmax posterior \(q_\phi(c_i=k\mid z_i,I)\), and Gumbel-softmax is used for categorical assignments. Thresholding a categorical probability vector can produce multiple labels at inference time, but the generative model and variational objective do not actually model independent or multi-label community memberships. With the stated \(\epsilon=0.3\), the softmax normalization also implies that a node can receive at most three communities, and possibly none if all probabilities are below threshold. Thus the empirical claim may be true for these datasets, but the wording that VECODER “learn[s] multiple community assignments” is stronger than what the formal latent-variable model directly supports. A Bernoulli/multilabel formulation or a clearer statement that overlap is obtained by a post-hoc thresholding heuristic would resolve this.

comments.title: Claim that all second-best methods are joint is contradicted by the Jaccard table
comments.quote: Third, it is noted that all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. This supports our claim that treating the two tasks jointly results in better performance.
comments.explanation: The intended observation is reasonable: many of the strongest baselines in Tables 2 and 3 are joint embedding/community-detection methods such as vGraph, ComE, CNRL, and ComGAN. I checked the table values to see whether the statement holds uniformly. It appears not to: in Table 3, for fb348, VECODER has the best Jaccard score at 43.5, while DGI has 41.77, which is higher than vGraph’s 41.0 and vGraph+’s 40.5. DGI, as described earlier, is an embedding method followed by clustering, not a joint community-detection and node-representation model in the sense claimed here. Therefore the universal statement “all the methods” is false for at least this case. Even aside from that exception, the inference that this observation supports the causal claim that joint treatment yields better performance is somewhat stronger than the table alone establishes.

comments.title: Count of best competing F1 methods omits one dataset
comments.quote: That is, excluding VECODER , the best performance is achieved by vGraph/vGraph+ on 5, ComGAN on 4 and ComE on 3 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score.
comments.explanation: I checked this count against Table 2 because the sentence is meant to summarize the lack of a consistently dominant competing baseline. The counts for vGraph/vGraph+ on 5 datasets, ComGAN on 4, and ComE on 3 are correct, but they sum to only 12 of the 13 datasets. The missing case appears to be Amazon, where CNRL has the best non-VECODER F1 score at 53.5, slightly above vGraph at 53.3 and vGraph+ at 53.2. The broader qualitative point still holds, but the enumeration is incomplete and could mislead readers into thinking those three methods account for all 13 cases.

comments.title: Overlapping-community F1 and Jaccard metrics are not sufficiently specified
comments.quote: Table 2: F1 scores for overlapping communities. Best and second best values are bold and blue respectively.
comments.explanation: The tables clearly report F1 and Jaccard percentages for overlapping-community detection, and it is plausible that the authors are following the evaluation protocol of Sun et al. (2019), since many baseline values are taken from that work. Still, for overlapping community detection, “F1 score” and “Jaccard similarity” are not uniquely defined: papers may use pairwise node co-membership measures, per-community matching with maximum bipartite alignment, micro/macro averaging over communities, or SNAP-style ground-truth matching. These choices can materially change the reported numbers, especially when the number and size distribution of communities vary. The passage and surrounding settings state the metric names but do not define the exact computation or matching/averaging protocol. Because the empirical comparison is central to the paper, this is a reproducibility gap unless the omitted supplementary material or code gives an exact metric implementation.

comments.title: Empirical “outperforms” claims lack uncertainty despite close margins
comments.quote: First, we note that our proposed method VECODER outperforms the competitive methods on all datasets in terms of Jaccard Similarity.
comments.explanation: The statement is correct if interpreted as comparing the reported mean values in Table 3: VECODER has the largest listed Jaccard score on every dataset. However, the paper states elsewhere that results are averaged over five runs, while the table gives no standard deviations, confidence intervals, or significance tests. Some margins are small, for example fb0 has 24.7 for VECODER versus 24.1 for ComGAN, and fb348 has 43.5 versus 41.77 for DGI. In empirical graph representation work, run-to-run variance from initialization, sampling, and train/test splits can be nontrivial. Thus “outperforms” is acceptable as a statement about table means, but the strength of the conclusion would be better supported by reporting variability or statistical significance, especially where margins are narrow.

comments.title: MNMF is grouped with non-joint baselines despite being a joint embedding/community method
comments.quote: First, we observe that MNMF, DeepWalk, LINE and Node2Vec provide a good baseline for the task. However, these methods are not able to achieve comparable performance on any dataset relative to the frameworks that treat the two tasks jointly.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a broad contrast between MNMF/DeepWalk/LINE/Node2Vec and methods that jointly learn representations and communities. That contrast is appropriate for DeepWalk, LINE, and Node2Vec, which are primarily node-embedding methods followed by a clustering step. However, MNMF/M-NMF is generally presented as a joint matrix-factorization approach for network embedding and community detection, and the paper’s own baseline description emphasizes that MNMF learns community membership distributions via a joint NMF objective with modularity regularization. Thus, including MNMF in “these methods” and then contrasting the group against “frameworks that treat the two tasks jointly” is misleading. The authors may only intend to say that MNMF and the simple embedding baselines are weaker than more recent joint frameworks, but as written it blurs an important methodological distinction among the baselines.

comments.title: Thresholding a categorical probability vector can yield no community, not just the most probable one
comments.quote: This is because the datasets contain overlapping communities and a very high _ϵ_ will cause the algorithm to give only the most probable community assignment instead of potentially providing multiple communities per node.
comments.explanation: The intuition that a larger threshold reduces the number of predicted communities is correct. However, under Eq. (14), the predicted overlapping community set is defined as all communities whose softmax probability is at least _ϵ_. Since these probabilities sum to one, if _ϵ > 0.7_ there can be at most one selected community, but there need not be any selected community unless the maximum probability also exceeds the threshold. This matters especially when _K_ is large, such as amazon1000 with _K=1000_, where a posterior distribution that is not extremely peaked could easily have all entries below the threshold. The passage therefore slightly overstates what the rule guarantees. If the implementation uses a fallback such as always assigning the argmax when the thresholded set is empty, that should be stated; otherwise the inference procedure can produce empty assignments, which affects reproducibility and interpretation of the sensitivity curve.

comments.title: Average community size for amazon1000 is incorrectly inferred as N/K despite overlapping communities
comments.quote: This effect is most visible in _amazon1000_ dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities.
comments.explanation: The number 1.54 appears to come from the table values _N=1540_ and _K=1000_, i.e. _N/K_. I checked whether this resolves the statement, but for an overlapping-community dataset this is not the usual average community size. The average number of node memberships per community should be the total number of node-community memberships divided by _K_, not the number of distinct nodes divided by _K_. Because nodes may belong to multiple selected Amazon communities, and because the dataset construction selects 1000 communities from the Amazon ground-truth communities, _N/K_ does not by itself establish that each community has only 1.54 members on average. The downstream explanation—that neighbors are likely to be in different communities because communities are so small—may still be plausible, but it is not justified by the stated calculation. This should either use the actual average ground-truth community size after preprocessing or be phrased more cautiously.
